---
title: "Continuous Random Variables"
author: "Fei Ye"
date: '`r format(Sys.time(), "%B %Y")`'
output:
  litedown::html_format:
    keep_md: true
    meta:
      css: ["@default", "@snap", "meta-data/custom.css", "@npm/@xiee/utils/css/key-buttons.min.css,heading-anchor.min.css"]
      js: ["@snap", "@npm/@xiee/utils/js/key-buttons.min.js, external-link.min.js, heading-anchor.min.js", "meta-data/lastupdated.js"]
      # header-includes: ["meta-data/Hypothesis-config.html", "meta-data/mathjax-config.html"]
    options:
      includes: ["meta-data/Hypothesis-config.html", "meta-data/mathjax-config.html"]
      number_sections: true
      latex_math: true
      js_math:
        package: mathjax
        version: 3
        js: es5/tex-mml-chtml.js
---


```{r, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  comment="#", 
  fig.retina=2, 
  crayon.enabled = TRUE,
  out.width = "100%",   # use % so the grepl("%$") check is TRUE
  screenshot.force = FALSE
  )
set.seed(15)
library(knitr)
library(ggplot2)
library(latex2exp)
```

## Learning Goals

- Demonstrate understanding of characteristics of normal distributions.

- Calculate accurate probabilities of continuous random variables and interpret them in a variety of settings.

- Calculate the standardized value (or $z$-score).

---

## Distribution of a Continuous Random Variable

The probability distribution of a continuous random variable $X$ is characterized by its **probability density function** $f(X)$ satisfying that the probability $P(a\leq X\leq b)$ equals the area above the interval $[a, b]$ but under the graph of the density function $f(X)$ which is also called a **density curve**.

::: {.center}
```{r uniform_dist, echo=FALSE, fig.width=5, fig.asp=0.5, warning=FALSE, out.width = "40%", fig.cap = ""}
puniform <- ggplot(
    data = data.frame(x = c(-0.5, 0.5)), aes(x)
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dunif,
    args = list(min = -0.5, max = 0.5),
    colour = "blue"
  ) +
  xlab("X") +
  ylab("") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=NULL) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(-0.35, 0, 0.35),
    labels =c( "a", "mean", "b" )
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun=dunif,
    args=list(min = -0.5, max = 0.5),
    xlim=c(-0.35, 0.35),
    geom="area",
    fill="#510bdd",
    alpha=0.2
  ) +
  ggtitle("A Uniform Distribution") +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = 0,
      y = 0,
      xend = 0,
      yend = 1,
    ),
    color="#389912",
    linetype="dashed"
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = -0.5,
      y = 0,
      xend = -0.5,
      yend = 1,
    ),
    linetype="dashed"
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = 0.5,
      y = 0,
      xend = 0.5,
      yend = 1,
    ),
    linetype="dashed"
  ) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24), panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(
      margin = margin(t = -20),
      hjust=0.95,
      size=18
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=28)
  ) +
  annotate(
    geom="text",
    x=0,
    y=0.5,
    label=TeX("P(a \\leq X < b)", output="character"),
    parse=TRUE,
    size = 8
  )

puniform
```
```{r normal_dist, echo=FALSE, fig.width=5, fig.asp=0.5, warning=FALSE, out.width = "40%", fig.cap = ""}
pnormal <- ggplot(
    data = data.frame(x = c(-3, 3)), aes(x)
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = 0, sd = 1),
    colour = "blue"
  ) +
  xlab("X") +
  ylab("") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=NULL) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(-1.5, 0, 1.2),
    labels =c( "a", "mean", "b" )
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun=dnorm,
    args=c(0,1),
    xlim=c(-1.5, 1.2),
    geom="area",
    fill="#510bdd",
    alpha=0.2
  ) +
  ggtitle("A Normal Distribution") +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = 0,
      y = 0,
      xend = 0,
      yend = dnorm(0, 0, 1),
    ),
    color="#389912",
    linetype="dashed"
  ) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24), panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(
      margin = margin(t = -20),
      hjust=0.95,
      size=18
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=25)
  ) +
  annotate(
    geom="text",
    x=0,
    y=0.18,
    label=TeX("$P(a \\leq X < b)$", output='character'),
    parse=TRUE,
    size = 8
  )

pnormal
```
:::

---

## Properties of Distribution of a Continuous Variable

- The probability density function $f$ is nonnegative, that is $f(X)\ge 0$.

- The total area under a density curve is 1.

- The cumulative probability $P(X\le b)$ of a random variable $X$ equals the area under the density curve to the left side of $b$.

- By the addition rule of probability, we have
  - $$P(a\le X\le b)=P(X\le b)-P(X\le a)$$
  - $$P(X\ge b)=1-P(X\le b)$$

- As a line segment has no area, we have $P(X\le a)=P(X< a)$ as well as $P(X\ge b)=P(X>b)$

---

## Example: An Uniform Distribution

Let $X$ be the amount of time that a commuter must wait for a train. Suppose $X$ has a probability density function
$$
f(X)=
\begin{cases}
  0.1, & 0\leq X\leq 10\\
  0,   & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

What is the probability that the commuter's waiting time is less than 4 minutes?

:::: {.row}
::: {.pull-left}
**Solution:** The probability $P(X\leq 4)$ is the area under the horizontal line $y=0.1$ to the left of $X=4$. Since $f(X)=0$ for $X<0$, the area is the area of the rectangle with width 4 and height 0.1. So the probability is $P(X\leq 4)=0.1\cdot 4=0.4$.
:::

::: {.pull-right}
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.asp=0.5, fig.align="center", warning=FALSE}
puniform <- ggplot(
    data = data.frame(x = c(0, 10)), aes(x)
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dunif,
    args = list(min = 0, max = 10),
    xlim=c(0, 10),
    colour = "blue"
  ) +
  xlab("X") +
  ylab("Density") +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = c(0,0),
    limits = c(0, 10.5),
    breaks = seq(0,10,2)
    # labels =c( "0", "4", "10" )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = c(0,0),
    limits = c(0, 0.2)
  )+
  stat_function(
    fun=dunif,
    args=list(min = 0, max = 10),
    xlim=c(0, 4),
    geom="area",
    fill="#510bdd"
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = 4,
      y = 0,
      xend = 4,
      yend = 0.1
    ),
    color="blue",
    linewidth=1.25
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = 0,
      y = 0.1,
      xend = 4,
      yend = 0.1
    ),
    color="blue",
    linewidth=1.25
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = 0,
      y = 0,
      xend = 4,
      yend =0
    ),
    color="blue",
    linewidth=2
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(
      x = 0,
      y = 0,
      xend = 0,
      yend = 0.1
    ),
    color="blue",
    linewidth=2
  ) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.line = element_line(),
    # plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24),
    panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_text(
      # hjust=0.95,
      size=18
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=28)
  ) +
  annotate(
    geom='text',
    x=2,
    y=0.05,
    label=TeX("$P( X\\leq 4)$",
              output='character'),
    parse=TRUE,
    size = 8
  )

puniform
```
:::
::::

---

## Normal Distribution

- A **normal distribution** has a **density function** $f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}},$
where $\mu$ is the mean, $\sigma$ is the standard deviation, $\pi\approx `r round(pi, 5)`$ and $e\approx `r round(exp(1), 5)`$. The graph of $f$ is called a **normal curve**.

- We write $X\sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ for a normal random variable $X$ with the mean $\mu$ and the standard deviation $\sigma$.

- A normal distribution has the following properties:
  - *The mean, median, and mode are equal*.
  - The normal curve is *bell shaped and __symmetric__* with respect to the mean.
  - The *total area* under the curve and above the $x$-axis is $1$.
  - The normal curve *approaches, but never touches, the $x$-axis* as $x$ goes to $\pm\infty$.
  <!-- - Between $\mu-\sigma$ and $\mu+\sigma$, the graph *curves downward*. On the left side of $\mu-\sigma$ or the right side of $\mu+\sigma$, the graph *curves upward*.   -->
  <!-- - A point at which the curve changes the direction of curving is called an **inflection point**. -->

---

## Normal Curves with Different Means and Standard Deviations

::: {.center}
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=10, fig.asp=0.5, warning=FALSE}
m1 <-0
m2 <- 1
m3 <- -1.5
mn <- c(m1, m2, m3)
pmean <- ggplot(
    data = data.frame(x = c(-4, 4)), aes(x)
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = m1, sd = 1),
    aes(colour = "B"),
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = m2, sd = 1),
    aes(colour = "G"),
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = m3, sd = 1),
    aes(colour = "R"),
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=NULL) +
  theme_bw() +
  ggtitle(TeX('Normal Curves with $\\sigma = 1$')) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0)+
  annotate("segment", 
           x = m1, 
           xend = m1, 
           y = 0, 
           yend = dnorm(m1, m1, 1), 
           color = "blue", 
           linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", 
           x = m2, 
           xend = m2, 
           y = 0, 
           yend = dnorm(m2, m2, 1), 
           color = "red", 
           linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", 
           x = m3, 
           xend = m3, 
           y = 0, 
           yend = dnorm(m3, m3, 1), 
           color = "red", 
           linetype = "dashed") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24), panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(
      margin = margin(t = -20),
      hjust=0.95,
      size=24
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=25),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 18)
  ) +
    scale_colour_manual(
    "",
    values= c("B"="blue", "R"="red", "G"="green"),
    labels = lapply(sprintf('$\\mu = %.1f$', mn), TeX)
  )

pmean
```
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=10, fig.asp=0.5}
sd1 <- 0.6
sd2 <- 1.5
sd3 <- 2
sd <- c(sd1, sd2, sd3)
psd <- ggplot(
    data = data.frame(x = c(-4, 4), y=c(0, 1)), aes(x, y)
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = 0, sd = sd1),
    aes(color="B"),
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = 0, sd = sd2),
    aes(color="G"),
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = 0, sd = sd3),
    aes(color="R"),
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=NULL) +
  theme_bw() +
  ggtitle(TeX('Normal Curves with $\\mu = 0$')) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0)+
  annotate("segment",
      x = 0,
      y = 0,
      xend = 0,
      yend = dnorm(0, 0, sd1),
    color="blue",
    linetype="dashed"
  ) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24), panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(
      margin = margin(t = -20),
      hjust=0.95,
      size=24
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=25),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    "",
    values= c("B"="blue", "R"="red", "G"="green"),
    labels = lapply(
      sprintf('$\\sigma = %.1f$', sd),TeX
    )
  )

psd
```

:::

---

## The Empirical Rule for Normal Distributions

For any normal distribution, the proportion of data values within 1, 2, and 3 standard deviations away from the mean are approximately 68.3%, 95.4% and 99.7% respectively.

::: {.center}
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=10, fig.asp=0.6}
sgma=1.5
br <- c(-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3)*sgma
lb <- c(0.683,0.954,0.997)

ms <- ggplot(data = data.frame(x = c(-6, 6)), aes(x)) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = 0, sd = 1.5),
    color="blue",
    linewidth = 1.25,
  ) +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks=br,
    labels=c(
      expression(mu-3*sigma),
      expression(mu-2*sigma),
      expression(mu-sigma),
      expression(mu),
      expression(mu+sigma),
      expression(mu+2*sigma),
      expression(mu+3*sigma)
    )
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  ggtitle(
    TeX('Area Under a Normal Curve Over $\\[ \\mu - n\\sigma, \\mu + n\\sigma \\]$')
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24),
    panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(
      margin = margin(t = -20),
      hjust=0.95,
      size=24,
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=20),
  )

for (i in br) {
  if( i != 0){
    ms <- ms + annotate(
      "segment",
      x = i,
      y = 0,
      xend = i,
      yend = (dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06*abs(i)/sgma),
      color="blue",
    )
  }
}

ms <- ms +  annotate(
   "segment",
      x = 0,
      y = 0,
      xend = 0,
      yend = dnorm(0, 0, sgma),
    color="blue"
)

# ms <- ms +
#   geom_point(
#     aes(x=sgma, y=dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)),
#     size=3
#   ) +
#   geom_point(
#     aes(x=-sgma, y=dnorm(-sgma, 0, sgma)),
#     size=3
#   )

# ms <- ms + annotate(
#   "segment",
#   x = 2*sgma,
#   xend = sgma,
#   y = 0.23,
#   yend = dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma),
#   colour = "purple",
#   alpha=0.6,
#   arrow=arrow()
# )+
#   annotate(
#     "segment",
#     x = 2*sgma,
#     xend = -sgma,
#     y = 0.23,
#     yend = dnorm(-sgma, 0, sgma),
#     colour = "purple",
#     alpha=0.6,
#     arrow=arrow()
#   ) #+
  # annotate(
  #   geom='text',
  #   x=3.1,
  #   y=0.24,
  #   label="Inflection Points",
  #   color = "purple",
  #   size=6
  # )


ms <- ms +
   annotate(
   "segment",
      x=-sgma,
      y=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06),
      xend=sgma,
      yend=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06),
    color="blue",
  ) +
  geom_label(
      x=0,
      y=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06),
    label=lb[1]
  )

ms <- ms +
 annotate(
   "segment",
      x=-2*sgma,
      y=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06*2),
      xend=2*sgma,
      yend=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06*2),
    color="blue"
  ) +
  geom_label(
    x=0,
    y=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06*2),
    label=lb[2]
  )

ms <- ms +
 annotate(
   "segment",
      x=-3*sgma,
      y=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06*3),
      xend=3*sgma,
      yend=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06*3),
    color="blue",
  ) +
  geom_label(
    x=0,
    y=(dnorm(sgma, 0, sgma)+0.1+0.06*3),
    label=lb[3]
  )

ms

```

:::

---

## Example: Foot length (1 of 3)

Suppose that foot length of a randomly chosen adult male is a normal random variable with the mean $\mu=11$ and the standard deviation $\sigma=1.5$.

- How likely is a male's foot length to be smaller than 9.5 inches
- How likely is a male's foot length to be bigger than 8 inches

**Solution:** Let's first sketch the normal curve.

::: {.center}
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.asp=0.6}
footsigma <- 1.5
footmu <- 11
footbr <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)*footsigma+footmu

foot <- ggplot(
  data = data.frame(x = c(7, 16)), aes(x)
) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = footmu, sd = footsigma),
    color="blue",
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun=dnorm,
    args=c(footmu,footsigma),
    xlim=c(6, 9.5),
    geom="area",
    fill="#510bdd",
    alpha=0.2
  ) +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks=footbr,
    labels=footbr
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  geom_hline(yintercept=-0.01) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24), panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(
    margin = margin(t = -20),
    hjust=0.95,
    size=24
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=20)
  )

for (i in -1:1) {
    foot <- foot +  annotate(
      "segment",
      x = i*footsigma+footmu,
      y = -0.01,
      xend = i*footsigma+footmu,
      yend = dnorm(i*footsigma+footmu, footmu, footsigma),
      color="blue"
    )
}

foot +
 annotate(
   "segment",
      x=-footsigma+footmu,
      y=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
      xend=footsigma+footmu,
      yend=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
    color="blue",
  ) +
 annotate(
   "segment",
      x=-footsigma+footmu,
      y=(dnorm(footsigma+footmu, footmu, footsigma)),
      xend=-footsigma+footmu,
      yend=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
    color="blue",
  ) +
 annotate(
   "segment",
      x=footsigma+footmu,
      y=(dnorm(footsigma+footmu, footmu, footsigma)),
      xend=footsigma+footmu,
      yend=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
    color="blue",
  ) +
  geom_label(
      x=footmu,
      y=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
    label="0.683"
  )
```
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.asp=0.6}
footsigma <- 1.5
footmu <- 11
footbr <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)*footsigma+footmu

foot2 <- ggplot(
  data = data.frame(x = c(7, 16)), aes(x)
) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(mean = footmu, sd = footsigma),
    color="blue",
    linewidth = 1.25
  ) +
  stat_function(
    fun=dnorm,
    args=c(footmu,footsigma),
    xlim=c(8, 16),
    geom="area",
    fill="#510bdd",
    alpha=0.2
  ) +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks=NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks=footbr,
    labels=footbr
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  geom_hline(yintercept=-0.01) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size=24), panel.border = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(
      margin = margin(t = -20),
      hjust=0.95,
      size=24
    ),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size=20)
  )

for (i in c(-2, 0, 2) ) {
    foot2 <- foot2 +  annotate(
   "segment",
      x = i*footsigma+footmu,
      y = -0.01,
      xend = i*footsigma+footmu,
      yend = dnorm(i*footsigma+footmu, footmu, footsigma),
      color="blue"
    )
}

foot2+
 annotate(
   "segment",
      x=-2*footsigma+footmu,
      y=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
      xend=2*footsigma+footmu,,
    color="blue",
  ) +
   annotate(
   "segment",
      x=-2*footsigma+footmu,
      y=(dnorm(2*footsigma+footmu, footmu, footsigma)),
      xend=-2*footsigma+footmu,
      yend=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
    color="blue",
  ) +
   annotate(
   "segment",
      x=2*footsigma+footmu,
      y=(dnorm(2*footsigma+footmu, footmu, footsigma)),
      xend=2*footsigma+footmu,
      yend=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
    color="blue",
  ) +
  geom_label(
      x=footmu,
      y=(dnorm(footmu, footmu, footsigma)+0.1),
    label="0.954"
  )
``` 
:::

---

## Example: Foot length (2 of 3)

**Method 1: Using the Empirical Rule**

Note that $9.5 = 11 - 1.5 = \mu - \sigma$. By the symmetry of normal curve, we know that the probability $P(X<9.5)$ is the shaded area on the left.
Because the probability of getting a foot length within 1 standard deviation away from the mean is 0.683. Then
$$P(X<9.5)=\frac12(1-P(9.5<X<12.5))\approx\frac12(1-0.683)=0.1585.$$
Note that $8=11-2\cdot 1.5=\mu-2\sigma$. Because the probability of getting a foot length within 2 standard deviation away from the mean is 0.954. Then
$$P(X>8)=(1-P(X<8))=1-\frac12(1- P(8<X<14))=1-\frac12(1-0.954)=0.977.$$

---

## Example: Foot length (3 of 3)

**Method 2: Using Excel**

The probability $P(X<x)$ for a normal random variable $X$ can be calculated using the Excel function `NORM.DIST(x, mean, sd, TRUE)`.

In this case, $P(X<9.5)=$ `=NORM.DIST(9.5, 11, 1.5, TRUE)` $\approx 0.1587.$

Since $P(X>8) = 1 - P(X < 8)$ and $P(X < 8)=$ `NORM.DIST(8, 11, 1.5, TRUE)`, it follows that
$$P(X>8) \approx 1 - 0.02275 = 0.97725.$$

---

## Standard Normal Distribution

- A normal distribution is called a **standard normal distribution** if the mean is $\mu=0$ and the standard deviation is $\sigma=1$.

- A random normal variable can be **standardized** by the following formula $z=\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}.$ We call the value $z$ the $Z$-**score** of $x$. In Excel, the $Z$-score of $x$ can be calculated using the function `STANDARDIZE()`.
  
- Standardization preserves probability:
  $$\textstyle P(a<X<b)=P\left(\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma}< Z < \frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right).$$

- The probability $P(Z< z)$ of a standard normal random variable $Z$ can be found using the Excel function `NORM.S.DIST(z, TRUE)` or the [standard normal distribution table](tables/normal-tables.html).

- Recall that the probability $P(X< x)$ of a normal random variable $X$ can be calculated using the Excel function `NORM.DIST(x, mean, sd, TRUE)`.

---

## Example: Standardization Revisited

Let $X$ be a normal random variable with the mean $\mu = 8$ and the standard deviation $\sigma=2$.

1. Find the $Z$-score for the value $X=13$.
2. Find the $X$-value for the $Z$-score $z=-0.6$.

**Solution:** The $z$-score for the value $X=13$ is
$$z=\dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}=\dfrac{13-8}{2}=\dfrac{5}{2}=2.5.$$

The $X$-value for the the $Z$-score $z=-0.6$ is
$$x=z\cdot\sigma+\mu=-0.6\cdot 2+8=-1.2+8=6.8.$$

---

## Example: Probabilities of Standard Normal Variables

Let $Z$ be a standard normal random variable.

<!-- :::: {.row} -->
<!-- ::: {.pull-left} -->
1. Find $P(Z<1.21)$.
2. Find $P(Z\geq 1.21)$.
3. Find $P(0<Z\leq 1.21)$.
<!-- ::: -->
<!-- ::: {.pull-right} -->
<!-- | Z   | 0      | 0.01   | 0.02   | -->
<!-- |-----|--------|--------|--------| -->
<!-- | 1.2 | <span class="red">0.8849</span> | 0.8869 | 0.8888 | -->
<!-- | 1.3 | 0.9856 | 0.9856 | 0.9857 | -->
<!-- ::: -->
<!-- :::: -->

**Solution:** To find the probability of getting a value less than $z$, we may use the Excel function `NORM.S.DIST(z,TRUE)`.

1. In this case, $P(Z<1.21)$ `=NORM.S.DIST(1.21, TRUE)` $\approx `r round(pnorm(1.21,0,1),4)`$.
2. Since the total area under the normal curve is 1, 
   $$P(Z\geq 1.21) = 1 - P(Z < 1.21) \approx 1-`r round(pnorm(1.21,0,1),4)`=`r round(1-pnorm(1.21,0,1),4)`.$$
3. By the symmetry, $P(Z<0)=0.5$ and
   $$P(0<Z<1.21) = P(Z < 1.21) - P(Z < 0) \approx `r round(pnorm(1.21,0,1),4)`-0.5=`r round(pnorm(1.21,0,1)-0.5,4)`.$$

---

## Example: Heights of 25-year-old women

The heights of 25-year-old women in a certain region are approximately normally distributed with mean 62 inches and standard deviation 4 inches. Find the probability that a randomly selected 25-year-old woman is more than 67 inches tall.

**Solution:** Let $X$ be the height of a randomly selected 25-year-old. From the question, $X$ is approximately normally distributed with mean 62 inches and standard deviation 4 inches. Since $P(X>67)=1-P(X\leq 67)$, to find $P(X>67)$, we find $P(X\leq 67)$ first.

**Method 1: Using `NORM.DIST(x, mean, SD, TRUE)`**

$P(X\le 67)$ `NORM.DIST(67, 62, 4, TRUE)` $\approx `r round(pnorm(67, 62, 4), 4)`$.

**Method 2: Using `NORM.S.DIST(z, TRUE)`**

The $Z$-score is $z=\frac{67-62}{4}=1.25$ and $P(X\le 67)=P(Z<1.25)=$ `NORM.S.DIST(1.25,TRUE)` $\approx `r round(pnorm(1.25, 0, 1), 4)`$.

Therefore, $P(X>67) \approx 1-`r round(pnorm(1.25, 0, 1), 4)`=`r round(1-pnorm(1.25, 0, 1), 4)`.$

---

## Cutoff Value for a Given Tail Area

- The $k$-th percentile for a random variable $X$ is the value $x_k$ that cuts off a left tail with the area $k/100$, that is $P(X<x_k)=\frac{k}{100}$, where $0\leq k\leq 100$.

- Let $c$ be a nonnegative number less than 0.5. The $(100c)$-th percentile for the standard normal distribution is usually denoted as $-z_c$, that is $P(Z<-z_c)=c<0.5$. By symmetry, $z_c$ is the value such that $P(Z> z_c)=c$, that is $P(Z<z_c)=1-c$.
  - **Note:** the cutoff value $z_c$ is positive if $c<0.5$.
  - In Excel, if $c<0.5$, the cutoff value $z_c$ such that $P(Z > z_c)=c$ can be calculated using the function `NORM.S.INV(1-c)`.

- For a normal random variable $X$ with the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the cutoff value $x^*$ with a **tail area** $p$, can be calculated using the Excel function `NORM.INV(p, mean, sd)`, or the standardization formula, that is, $x^*=z^*\cdot \sigma+\mu,$ where $z^*$ is the cutoff $z$-score with the tail area $p$, that is $z^*=$ `NORM.S.INV(p)`.

---

## Example: Cutoff Value

Let $X$ be the normal random variable with mean $6$ and standard deviation $3$. Suppose the value $x^*$ cuts off a left-tail area $0.05$. Find the value $x^*$.

**Solution:** Since the $x^*$ cuts off a left-tail, to find the value $x^*$:

**Method 1:** $x^*=$ `NORM.INV(0.05, 6, 3)` $\approx `r round(qnorm(0.05, 6, 3), 3)`.$

**Method 2:** Let $z^*$ be the cutoff $z$-score with the tail area $0.05$. Then $z^*=$ `NORM.S.INV(0.05)` $\approx `r round(qnorm(0.05, 0, 1), 3)`.$ Then $$x^*=z^*\cdot \sigma+\mu=`r round(qnorm(0.05, 0, 1), 3)`\cdot 3+6=`r round(qnorm(0.05, 0, 1), 3)*3+6`.$$

---

## Example: Math Course Placement

Scores on a standardized college placement examination are normally distributed with mean 60 and standard deviation 13. Students whose scores are in the top $5\%$ will be placed in a Calculus II course. Find the minimum score needed to be placed in a Calculus II course.

**Solution:** Let $x^*$ be the minimum score. From the question, we know that $P(X\geq x^*)=5\%$. Equivalently, $P(X<x^*)=1 - 5\%$.

**Method 1:** $x^*=$ `NORM.INV(1-5%, 60, 13)` $\approx `r round(qnorm(0.95, 60, 13), 2)`.$

**Method 2:** Let $z^*$ be the cutoff $z$-score with the tail area $1-5%$. Then $z^*=$ `NORM.S.INV(1-5%)` $\approx `r round(qnorm(0.95, 0, 1), 2)`.$ Then $$x^*=z^*\cdot \sigma+\mu=`r round(qnorm(0.95, 0, 1), 2)`\cdot 13+60=`r round(qnorm(0.95, 0, 1), 2)*13+60`.$$

So the minimum score needed to be placed in a Calculus II course is $`r ceiling(qnorm(0.95, 60, 13))`$.

::: {.footmark}
  **Note:** The minimum score is the same as the 95th percentile.
:::

---

## Practice: Dash washing time {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=7251&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

## Practice: Fruit weight {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=7280&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

## Practice: Shortest lifespan {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=7281&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

## Practice: Battery life {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=268987&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

## Practice: Sum of Normal Random Variables {.unnumbered}

Let $Z$ be a normal random variable with $\mu=0$ and $\sigma=1$. Let $X$ be a normal random variable with $\mu=4.3$ and $\sigma=1.7$.

Determine the values $P(Z>1) + P(X<6)$ and explain how do you find the value.

---

<!--# class="middle center" -->

::: {.part}
Extra Practice
:::

---

## Practice: Area under a normal curve {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=267521&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

## Practice: Find cutoff $Z$-scores {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=7277&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

## Practice: Chocolate Chips in Acceptable Cookies {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=293469&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

## Practice: Blood Pressure {.unnumbered}

::: {.iframecontainer}
`r knitr::include_url("https://www.myopenmath.com/embedq2.php?id=283090&seed=2020&showansafter", height="560px")`
:::

---

<!--# class="middle center" -->

::: {.part}
Lab Instructions in Excel
:::

---

## Excel Functions for Normal Distributions

- Let $Z$ be a standard normal random varaible. In Excel, $P(Z<z)$ is given by `NORM.S.DIST(z, TRUE)`.

- Let $X$ be a normal random variable with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, that is $X\sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. In Excel, $P(X<x)$ is given by `NORM.DIST(x, mean, sd, TRUE)`.

- When a cumulative probability $p=P(X<x)$ of a normal random variable $X$ is given, we can find $x$ using `NORM.INV(p, mean, sd)`.

- When a cumulative probability $p=P(Z<z)$ of a standard normal random variable $Z$ is given, we can find $z$ using `NORM.S.INV(p)`.

---

## Lab Practice: Normal Variable

1. Let $Z$ be a standard normal random variable. Find the probabilities:
  $$\text{1.}\,\, P(Z<1.58)\quad \text{2.}\,\,  P(-0.6<Z<1.67)\quad \text{3.}\,\, P(Z>0.19).$$

2. Let $X$ be a normal random variable with $\mu=5$ and $\sigma=2$. Find the probabilities:
  $$\text{1.}\,\,  P(-2<X<8)\quad \text{2.}\,\, P(X>-1) \quad \text{3.}\,\, P(X<4).$$
